excerpt: Mining hardware progressed from general-purpose CPUs to GPUs, then to FPGAs, and finally to ASICs. Each step represented an order-of-magnitude improvement in hashes per joule. ASICs are the end of this progression for SHA256: no further specialization is possible because the workload is already a fixed primitive.
body:
Source excerpt:
Mining hardware progressed from general-purpose CPUs to GPUs, then to FPGAs, and finally to ASICs. Each step represented an order-of-magnitude improvement in hashes per joule. ASICs are the end of this progression for SHA256: no further specialization is possible because the workload is already a fixed primitive.

---

Cited by leaf "ASICs and the mining-hardware industry":
Mining moved from CPUs to GPUs to FPGAs to ASICs — application-specific integrated circuits that do nothing except SHA256 — within Bitcoin's first four years, and ASICs have been dominant since 2013. The transition was inevitable: SHA256 is a fixed-function workload with no need for a general-purpose CPU, so any sufficiently large reward eventually justifies someone designing custom silicon for it [1]. The relevant question stopped being "can ASICs be avoided" and became "is the ASIC market itself competitive."

It's more competitive than people often assume. Bitmain still has the largest market share, but MicroBT (Whatsminer), Canaan, and a handful of newer entrants like Auradine and Block's Proto reference design now ship at competitive efficiency, and reverse-engineered firmware (Braiins OS, Vnish) further loosens any one vendor's grip [2]. A given generation's flagship miner usually has two or three credible alternatives within a few months. Concentration risk is real but it lives in a market structure that responds to demand, not in cryptographic lock-in.

The more honest concern is geographic concentration of where miners physically operate. Hashrate distribution shifted dramatically when China banned mining in 2021 — the US went from a small share to roughly 35–40% of global hashrate, with the remainder spread across Russia, Kazakhstan, Canada, Paraguay, Bhutan, Ethiopia, and a long tail of stranded-energy sites [3]. That distribution is itself a competitive market: capital chases the cheapest reliable kilowatt, and policy regimes that punish miners simply move the hashrate to ones that don't. Coordinated capture of physical mining at the global level has no historical precedent and would require simultaneous action by mutually-hostile jurisdictions.

---

Cited by leaf "The difficulty adjustment":
Every 2,016 blocks — roughly two weeks — the protocol looks at how long the previous 2,016 blocks took to find and retargets the difficulty so that, going forward, blocks should land every ten minutes on average [1]. If the last epoch took less than two weeks, difficulty goes up; if it took more, it goes down. The clamp is hard-coded to ±4× per adjustment so a sudden hashrate swing can't run away in a single epoch.

The mechanism is what makes Bitcoin's issuance schedule predictable. Block subsidy halves every 210,000 blocks, which is exactly 100 epochs, so the supply curve is timed to wall-clock years only because the difficulty adjustment keeps blocks landing at the target rate regardless of how much hashrate joins or leaves [2]. When China kicked out miners in mid-2021 and roughly half the hashrate went offline overnight, blocks slowed to ~14 minutes for two weeks, then the adjustment dropped difficulty by 28% and the chain returned to ten minutes — no manual intervention, no developer call, no governance vote.

The edge cases are worth knowing. The retarget reads timestamps from block headers, which miners can lie about within bounds set by other rules (median-of-eleven past blocks, two hours into the future); this is exploited by a known-but-impractical "timewarp" attack that has never been used [3]. There is also a long-running off-by-one in the exact window the calculation reads (2,015 blocks not 2,016) — a known quirk preserved for backward compatibility, not a bug worth fixing.

---

Cited by leaf "Pool dynamics and Stratum V2":
A miner running a single ASIC has to wait years between blocks on average even at modern difficulty, so almost all hashrate joins a pool: many miners contribute hashrate, the pool finds blocks roughly proportionally to its total share, and rewards get split out per contributed work [1]. The economics of variance reduction are real and not going away — the only question is what the pool operator decides on behalf of its miners, and that question has been the active reform area for the last few years.

Under Stratum V1 — the protocol most pools still use — the pool operator builds the block template: which transactions go in, in what order, and which addresses receive the coinbase and miner-fee outputs. Individual miners just hash whatever the pool sends them. This concentrates a real censorship and policy lever in the hands of a few dozen pool operators worldwide [2]. The well-publicized incidents of pools temporarily filtering specific transactions — for sanctions compliance, for spam policy, or under regulatory pressure — all played out at this layer.

Stratum V2 (BIP started 2019, now in production rollout) splits the protocol so individual miners can construct their own block templates and submit work against them, while the pool still handles payment accounting [3]. The pool is reduced to a payment processor; the policy choices about which transactions get included move back to whoever owns the hashrate. Adoption is partial and gradual — Braiins, Demand, and a handful of pools have shipped V2 endpoints; most hashrate still mines under V1 — but the architectural fix to the most-cited centralization concern is built and deployed, not theoretical.
